prescription-politics: Pfizer, Johnson & Johnson, Eli Lilly — three names on the pharmacy shelf, three political action committees writing checks in Washington. Big Pharma hedges both sides. Not equally.

The numbers below are federal campaign contributions for the 2024 cycle, as tracked by OpenSecrets.org: corporate PAC giving plus employees' individual donations to federal candidates. They are not direct corporate treasury gifts — those are illegal — and they don't include lobbying, which is a separate (and much larger) ledger.

You'll see three unlabeled donation profiles — party split and total spend. One hint before you start: only one of these three PACs tilts toward the party that votes against letting Medicare negotiate drug prices.
office-supplies: Best Buy, Staples, OfficeMax — the errand you run without thinking about it. But when it comes to federal campaign money, not every brand shows up to write a check.

The numbers below are 2024-cycle federal contributions tracked by OpenSecrets.org — corporate PAC giving plus employees' individual donations. One of these three made zero direct federal contributions in the entire cycle. That's not missing data; in a system where showing up is the norm, silence is its own statement.
Three profiles, unlabeled. One sat out the cycle — so the real puzzle is the other two: compare the totals.
americas-250th: Anheuser-Busch, Harley-Davidson, Levi Strauss — three brands that sell Americana itself. Stars, stripes, open roads, cold beer. But marketing isn't a political identity: born in the USA, banked elsewhere.

The numbers below are 2024-cycle federal contributions tracked by OpenSecrets.org — corporate PAC giving plus employees' individual donations to federal candidates, not corporate treasury gifts and not lobbying.
Three unlabeled profiles. One tilts one way, one tilts slightly the other, and one is nearly unanimous — and probably not the one the branding would have you guess.
